findings.description: parse() builds a column-name->index map (line 36) and validates required columns are present (lines 37-39), but then reads fields by hardcoded positional index (row[0..3]) instead of via that map, so any CSV whose columns are present but in a different order is silently mis-parsed (wrong field assignments) or crashes — the validation gives false confidence in correctness.

int(row[0]) and float(row[2]) (and row[3]) are unguarded: a single non-numeric value (e.g. empty 'amount', currency symbol, text 'id') raises ValueError, and a short row with fewer than 4 fields raises IndexError, aborting the entire batch on one malformed row with no per-row error handling or skip.
count_rows() returns len(lines) - 1, which yields -1 for an empty file (0 lines) — a nonsensical negative data-row count that callers will treat as a real value.

count_rows() counts physical newlines via readlines(), but parse() uses a real csv.reader; for any CSV containing RFC-4180 quoted fields with embedded newlines the two disagree (count_rows over-counts records), so count_rows is not a reliable row count for the same data parse() accepts.

open(path) in count_rows() is never closed (no context manager), leaking the file handle until GC; under CPython it usually closes promptly but is non-deterministic and will exhaust descriptors under high call volume.

export() derives the destination filename solely from os.path.basename(filename), so two distinct source paths sharing the same basename silently overwrite each other in the export dir, and an empty/trailing-slash filename yields basename '' which writes to (or errors on) the directory path itself.

The 'index' dict mapping header names to positions is computed but never used (dead code), which is the symptom of the positional-parse bug on line 44 — it was presumably intended to drive field lookup.

parse() returns [] for empty input but a CSV containing only a header row falls through the loop to also return [] with no error, while a header missing required columns raises — inconsistent contract for the 'no data rows' cases that callers may not anticipate.
